sections.title: What is Matter?
sections.content:
Matter is anything that occupies space, has mass, and can be perceived by our senses. Look around you -- everything you can see, touch, smell, or feel is matter! Air, water, iron, hydrogen, oxygen, milk, oil, sugar, salt -- all of these are matter. In fact, matter includes all the living and non-living things in the entire universe.

But what is matter made of? In ancient India, philosophers believed that matter was made up of five 'tatvas' (constituents): Akash (sky/space), Vayu (air), Tejas or Agni (fire), Jal (water), and Prithvi (earth). Later, the great Indian sage Maharishi Kanada proposed that matter is made up of tiny particles, which he called 'anu'. Today, science has confirmed that matter is composed of extremely tiny particles called molecules. A molecule is the smallest particle of a substance that can exist freely in nature and still has all the properties of that substance.

Molecules themselves are made up of even smaller particles called atoms. A molecule composed of just one atom is called a monoatomic molecule (like helium, neon, argon). A molecule made of two atoms is called a diatomic molecule (like hydrogen, oxygen, nitrogen). And a molecule made of more than two atoms is called a polyatomic molecule (like water, ammonia, carbon dioxide).

key_terms.definition: Anything that occupies space, has mass, and can be perceived by our senses.
The smallest particle of a substance that can exist freely in nature and has all the properties of that substance.
The smallest unit of an element; molecules are made up of atoms.
A molecule composed of a single atom (e.g., helium, neon, argon).
A molecule composed of two atoms (e.g., hydrogen molecule, oxygen molecule, nitrogen molecule).
A molecule composed of more than two atoms (e.g., water, ammonia, carbon dioxide).

fun_facts: Maharishi Kanada, an ancient Indian sage, proposed the idea of tiny particles making up matter long before modern scientists did. He called them 'anu' -- remarkably similar to the concept of atoms!
A single drop of water contains about 1.67 sextillion (1,670,000,000,000,000,000,000) water molecules!
examples: Air is matter -- even though you cannot see it, it occupies space (fills a balloon) and has mass.
Water is matter -- it occupies space in a glass and has mass that you can feel when you lift the glass.
A water molecule (H2O) is polyatomic because it is made of 3 atoms: 2 hydrogen atoms and 1 oxygen atom.

sections.title: Characteristics of Molecules
sections.content:
Molecules have four important characteristics that help us understand how matter behaves:

1. VERY SMALL IN SIZE: A molecule is incredibly tiny -- about 10^(-10) metre in size. That is 0.0000000001 metre! This is so small that even the most powerful microscope cannot show you an individual molecule. To give you an idea, if you lined up molecules side by side, you would need about 10 billion of them to cover just 1 metre.

2. HAVE SPACES BETWEEN THEM: The molecules in any substance are not jammed together with zero gap. There is always some space between them, called inter-molecular space. This space is least in solids, more in liquids, and maximum in gases. Here is a cool activity to prove this: Take 100 mL of water in a measuring cylinder. Add 20 grams of salt and stir well. You will notice that the water level does NOT rise! Where did the salt go? The salt molecules fit into the spaces between the water molecules. This proves that molecules have gaps between them.

3. IN CONSTANT MOTION: Molecules are never at rest -- they are always moving! But they move differently in the three states of matter. In solids, molecules vibrate back and forth about their fixed positions (like wiggling in place). In liquids, molecules move freely within the boundary of their container. In gases, molecules zoom around randomly in all directions, filling up whatever space is available. You can see evidence of this motion when you open a bottle of perfume in one corner of a room -- within minutes, the smell reaches every corner because the perfume molecules are constantly moving and spreading out. Another experiment: sprinkle lycopodium powder on the surface of water and watch it through a magnifying glass. You will see the powder particles dancing around in a zig-zag path. This random zig-zag motion is called Brownian motion, caused by water molecules bumping into the powder particles.

4. ATTRACT EACH OTHER: Molecules exert a force of attraction on each other. This force is called inter-molecular force. Importantly, this force is NOT caused by gravity (the mass of molecules is way too small for gravity to matter). Instead, it is an electrostatic force caused by the electric charges within the molecules. This force has a very short range -- it is effective only when molecules are within about 10^(-9) metre of each other. Beyond that distance, the force practically vanishes.

key_terms.definition: The space or gap between the molecules of a substance. It is least in solids, more in liquids, and maximum in gases.
The force of attraction between molecules of matter. It is an electrostatic force (not gravitational) and is effective only up to a separation of about 10^(-9) metre.
The random zig-zag movement of small particles suspended in a liquid or gas, caused by collisions with the molecules of the liquid or gas.

fun_facts: Brownian motion is named after the Scottish botanist Robert Brown, who first observed it in 1827 while looking at pollen grains dancing in water under a microscope.
The inter-molecular force between molecules is effective only up to 10^(-9) metre -- that is one-billionth of a metre, or about 10 times the size of an atom!
examples: Salt dissolving in water without increasing the water level: salt molecules occupy the inter-molecular spaces between water molecules.
Perfume spreading across a room: perfume molecules are in constant random motion and gradually spread everywhere.
Lycopodium powder on water surface showing zig-zag movement: water molecules constantly collide with the powder particles, pushing them in random directions (Brownian motion).

sections.title: Inter-molecular Forces: Cohesion and Adhesion
sections.content:
The inter-molecular force of attraction between molecules comes in two types, depending on whether the molecules belong to the same substance or different substances:

FORCE OF COHESION: This is the inter-molecular force of attraction between molecules of the SAME substance. For example, water molecules attract other water molecules, and iron molecules attract other iron molecules. Cohesion is what holds a substance together. It is responsible for properties like surface tension in liquids (which lets small insects walk on water!).

FORCE OF ADHESION: This is the force of attraction between molecules of TWO DIFFERENT substances. For example, the attraction between water molecules and glass molecules (which is why water climbs up the sides of a glass tube), or the attraction between glue molecules and paper molecules (which is why glue sticks things together).

The strength of these forces varies across the three states of matter:
- In SOLIDS, the force of attraction between molecules is very strong. This is why solids are rigid and hold their shape.
- In LIQUIDS, the force is less strong (moderate). This is why liquids can flow but still have a definite volume.
- In GASES, the force is negligible (almost zero). This is why gases spread out to fill any container.

Because the molecular forces in liquids and gases are weak, they can flow. Solids cannot flow because their molecular forces are too strong. Adhesives (like glue and tape) work because they have very strong molecular forces of attraction with the surfaces they stick to.

key_terms.definition: The inter-molecular force of attraction between molecules of the SAME substance.
The force of attraction between molecules of TWO DIFFERENT substances.
A property of liquids caused by cohesive forces, making the surface behave like a stretched elastic sheet.
A property of liquids related to inter-molecular forces that determines how easily a liquid flows. Honey has high viscosity; water has low viscosity.

fun_facts: Surface tension caused by cohesion allows small insects like water striders to walk on the surface of water without sinking!
Adhesion between water and glass molecules is why water rises in a thin glass tube -- this is called capillary action, and it is how plants transport water from their roots to their leaves.
examples: A drop of water holds together as a round drop because of cohesion between water molecules.
Glue sticks two pieces of paper together because of strong adhesion between glue molecules and paper molecules.
Water wets a glass surface because the adhesive force between water and glass is stronger than the cohesive force between water molecules.

sections.title: Three States of Matter
sections.content:
Matter exists in three physical states: solid, liquid, and gas. The state in which matter exists depends on physical conditions such as temperature. For example, water can exist in all three states: as ice (solid) below 0 degrees Celsius, as water (liquid) between 0 and 100 degrees Celsius, and as steam (gas) above 100 degrees Celsius.

SOLIDS are rigid. They have a definite shape and a definite volume (size). A brick stays brick-shaped no matter where you put it. You cannot compress a solid easily.

LIQUIDS are not rigid. They have a definite volume but NO definite shape. A liquid takes the shape of whatever container you pour it into. Pour water into a glass, and it becomes glass-shaped. Pour it into a bowl, and it becomes bowl-shaped. But the volume (amount) stays the same.

GASES are not rigid either. They have neither a definite shape nor a definite volume. A gas fills up the entire container it is placed in, no matter how big or small the container is. If you release gas from a small cylinder into a large room, the gas spreads out to fill the entire room.

The state of matter is determined by three properties:
1. Inter-molecular space -- the gap between molecules
2. Inter-molecular force -- the strength of attraction between molecules
3. Kinetic energy -- the energy of motion of the molecules

In a SOLID, the inter-molecular force is very strong and the kinetic energy of molecules is very low. So the molecules stay close together with very little space between them, and the substance is rigid.

In a LIQUID, the inter-molecular force is moderate and the kinetic energy is enough for molecules to move around within the substance. The inter-molecular space is more than in solids.

In a GAS, the inter-molecular force is negligible and the kinetic energy is very high. Molecules fly around freely with large spaces between them.

key_terms.definition: A state of matter with definite shape and definite volume. Molecules are closely packed with strong inter-molecular forces.
A state of matter with definite volume but no definite shape. Takes the shape of its container.
A state of matter with neither definite shape nor definite volume. Fills up the entire available space.
The energy a molecule possesses due to its motion. Higher kinetic energy means faster molecular motion.

fun_facts: Water is one of the few substances that naturally exists in all three states on Earth -- as ice (glaciers, ice caps), liquid water (oceans, rivers), and water vapour (clouds, humidity).
There is actually a fourth state of matter called plasma, found in stars, lightning, and neon signs. But at the Class 8 level, we study only three states.
examples: Ice keeps its shape whether it is on a plate or in a bowl -- it is a solid with definite shape and volume.
Water takes the shape of a glass, a bottle, or a puddle -- it is a liquid with definite volume but no definite shape.
When you inflate a balloon, the air fills the entire balloon evenly -- gas has neither definite shape nor definite volume.
Heating ice converts it to water (liquid), and further heating converts water to steam (gas) -- same substance, different states depending on temperature.

sections.title: Molecular Models of Solids, Liquids, and Gases
sections.content:
Scientists use molecular models to explain the behaviour of matter in each state. Let us look at how molecules are arranged and behave in each state:

MOLECULAR MODEL OF SOLIDS:
- Solids are made up of very tiny molecules that behave like tiny rigid balls.
- The separation between molecules (inter-molecular spacing) is very small -- molecules are packed closely together.
- Molecules can only vibrate to and fro about their mean (fixed) positions. They do NOT leave their positions. Think of it like people standing in a crowded bus -- they can sway slightly, but they cannot move around.
- The attractive forces between molecules are very strong, which is why molecules stay closely packed and the solid keeps its shape.

MOLECULAR MODEL OF LIQUIDS:
- Liquids are made up of very tiny molecules, but they are NOT arranged rigidly like in solids.
- The inter-molecular spaces are more than in solids -- molecules are loosely packed.
- Liquid molecules can move about freely within the boundary of the vessel. They slide past each other. Think of people moving around in a room -- they stay in the room but can walk anywhere inside it.
- The positions of molecules are not fixed because the inter-molecular forces are weaker compared to solids.

MOLECULAR MODEL OF GASES:
- Gas molecules can be thought of as rigid, homogeneous (uniform), and perfectly elastic balls. 'Perfectly elastic' means that when gas molecules collide, they bounce off without losing any energy.
- The separation between molecules is very large compared to liquids and solids.
- Molecules can move about freely in all directions, filling the entire space available to them. Think of a few people in a huge empty ground -- they can run anywhere they want.
- The inter-molecular forces are very weak (almost negligible), so molecules are wide apart and their positions are not fixed at all.

key_terms.definition: The average or central position about which a molecule in a solid vibrates back and forth.
A property of gas molecules meaning they bounce off each other during collisions without losing any kinetic energy.
The distance or gap between neighbouring molecules. Smallest in solids, larger in liquids, largest in gases.

fun_facts: In a solid like iron, each molecule vibrates about 10 trillion (10^13) times per second about its mean position!
Gas molecules move at incredible speeds -- oxygen molecules at room temperature travel at about 480 metres per second, which is faster than the speed of sound!
examples: A block of wood keeps its shape because its molecules are locked in fixed positions and can only vibrate -- this is the molecular model of a solid.
When you pour juice from a bottle into a glass, it flows and takes the shape of the glass because liquid molecules can slide past each other -- this is the molecular model of a liquid.
When you open a gas cylinder, the gas rushes out and fills the entire room because gas molecules move freely in all directions -- this is the molecular model of a gas.

sections.title: Comparison of Solids, Liquids, and Gases
sections.content: Here is a comprehensive comparison of solids, liquids, and gases across all important properties. Study this table carefully -- it summarises everything about the three states of matter in one place.

tables.title: Distinction Between Solids, Liquids, and Gases
rows: No definite shape (takes shape of container)
No definite shape (takes shape of container)
Vibrate to and fro about mean positions
Move freely within the boundary of the vessel
Move freely in all available space

tables.title: Common Examples of Solids, Liquids, and Gases

fun_facts: Glass appears to be a solid, but scientists have debated whether it is actually an extremely slow-flowing liquid! Very old glass windows are sometimes thicker at the bottom, though modern science suggests glass is technically an 'amorphous solid'.
examples: A brick (solid) has definite shape and volume. Water (liquid) has definite volume but takes the shape of whatever container it is in. Air (gas) fills the entire room it is in, with no definite shape or volume.

sections.title: Change of State -- Overview
sections.content:
Matter can change from one state to another. This happens when physical conditions (mainly temperature) change. The process of change from one state to another by absorption or rejection of heat at a constant temperature is called change of state.

There are six processes of change of state:

1. MELTING (or Fusion): Solid --> Liquid (by heating at a fixed temperature). Example: Ice melts to form water.

2. FREEZING: Liquid --> Solid (by cooling at a fixed temperature). Example: Water freezes to form ice.

3. BOILING (or Vaporization): Liquid --> Gas (by heating at a fixed temperature). Example: Water boils to form steam.

4. CONDENSATION: Gas --> Liquid (by cooling at a fixed temperature). Example: Steam condenses to form water.

5. SUBLIMATION: Solid --> Gas directly (without becoming liquid first). Example: Camphor directly turns into vapour.

6. DEPOSITION (or Solidification): Gas --> Solid directly (without becoming liquid first). Example: Carbon dioxide gas cools to form dry ice.

A very important point: During any change of state, the temperature remains CONSTANT even though heat is being absorbed or released. The heat energy is used to change the arrangement of molecules (overcoming inter-molecular forces) rather than increasing the temperature. This heat is called latent heat or hidden heat.

key_terms.definition: The process of changing from one physical state (solid, liquid, or gas) to another by absorption or rejection of heat at a constant temperature.
The change from solid to liquid state on heating at a fixed temperature.
The change from liquid to solid state on cooling at a fixed temperature.
The change from liquid to gaseous state on heating at a fixed temperature.
The change from gaseous state to liquid state on cooling at a fixed temperature.
The change from solid state directly to gaseous state without passing through the liquid state.
The change from gaseous state directly to solid state without passing through the liquid state.

fun_facts: The word 'latent' comes from the Latin word 'latere' meaning 'to lie hidden'. Latent heat is called 'hidden' because it does not show up as a temperature change!
examples: Ice melting in your glass of juice on a hot day: solid (ice) absorbs heat and changes to liquid (water) -- this is melting.
Water droplets forming on the outside of a cold bottle: water vapour in the air loses heat to the cold surface and changes to liquid -- this is condensation.
Moth balls (naphthalene balls) in your cupboard slowly shrinking and disappearing: the solid naphthalene directly changes to vapour -- this is sublimation.

sections.title: Melting and Freezing
sections.content:
MELTING: When you heat a solid, its temperature rises until it reaches a specific temperature where it starts changing into a liquid. This temperature is called the melting point. At the melting point, the solid keeps absorbing heat but the temperature does NOT rise -- all the heat energy goes into breaking the inter-molecular forces and separating the molecules. Only after all the solid has melted does the temperature start rising again.

For example, when you heat ice, the temperature rises until it reaches 0 degrees Celsius. At 0 degrees Celsius, the ice starts melting into water. The temperature stays at 0 degrees Celsius until ALL the ice has melted. Only then does the temperature of the water start rising.

Here is a cool experiment: Heat some wax in a test tube. The temperature rises to 55 degrees Celsius, then stays at 55 degrees Celsius while the wax melts. After all the wax has melted, the temperature starts rising again. So the melting point of wax is 55 degrees Celsius.

MOLECULAR EXPLANATION OF MELTING: In a solid, molecules are closely packed with strong forces of attraction, vibrating about their fixed positions with very little kinetic energy. When you heat the solid, the kinetic energy of molecules increases and they vibrate more and more violently. At the melting point, the molecules finally have enough kinetic energy to overcome the forces of attraction holding them in place. They break free, move apart, and become free to move around -- the solid becomes a liquid. The heat absorbed during melting does NOT increase kinetic energy (that is why temperature stays constant). Instead, it increases the potential energy of molecules by increasing the separation between them.

FREEZING: This is the reverse of melting. When you cool a liquid, it loses heat and its temperature drops until it reaches the freezing point, where it starts changing into a solid. The temperature stays constant during freezing until all the liquid has frozen.

IMPORTANT: For any substance, the melting point and the freezing point are the SAME temperature. Ice melts at 0 degrees Celsius, and water freezes at 0 degrees Celsius. Wax melts at 55 degrees Celsius, and liquid wax freezes at 55 degrees Celsius.

Also, the amount of heat released during freezing is exactly equal to the amount of heat absorbed during melting (for the same mass of substance). The heat absorbed or released per unit mass during a change of state is called specific latent heat.

key_terms.definition: The temperature at which a solid changes into a liquid without any further increase in temperature.
The temperature at which a liquid changes into a solid without any further decrease in temperature. It is the same as the melting point for any substance.
The amount of heat absorbed or released during a change of state per unit mass of the substance, measured in joules per kilogram (J/kg).

tables.title: Melting Points and Heat Absorbed for Common Substances
headers: Heat Absorbed (Specific Latent Heat of Fusion)

fun_facts: Water EXPANDS when it freezes into ice -- this is unusual because most substances (like wax, lead) CONTRACT when they freeze. This is why ice floats on water, and why water pipes can burst in extremely cold weather!
The melting point of ice DECREASES when you increase pressure. But the melting point of wax INCREASES with more pressure. Different substances behave differently!
Adding salt to ice lowers its melting point. This salt-ice mixture is called a freezing mixture and is used to make Kulfis and ice cream!
Copper has an incredibly high melting point of 1085 degrees Celsius -- that is why copper vessels can be used for cooking without melting.
examples: Ice at 0 degrees C absorbs 336 x 10^3 J of heat per kilogram to melt into water at 0 degrees C. The temperature stays at 0 degrees C throughout the melting process.
When making Kulfis, salt is added to ice to create a freezing mixture. The salt lowers the melting point of ice below 0 degrees C, making the mixture cold enough to freeze the Kulfi mixture.

sections.title: Vaporization and Boiling
sections.content:
When you heat a liquid, its temperature keeps rising until it reaches a specific temperature called the boiling point. At the boiling point, the liquid starts changing into gas (vapour). The temperature stays constant at the boiling point -- all the heat energy goes into converting the liquid into vapour, not into raising the temperature.

For example, when you heat water, the temperature rises steadily until it reaches 100 degrees Celsius. At 100 degrees Celsius, bubbles start forming vigorously throughout the water, and the water begins to change into steam. The temperature stays at 100 degrees Celsius until all the water has boiled away.

MOLECULAR EXPLANATION OF BOILING: In a liquid, molecules move in all directions within the boundary of the container, with small forces of attraction between them and moderate kinetic energy. When you heat the liquid, the average kinetic energy of molecules increases. At the boiling point, the molecules finally gain enough kinetic energy to completely overcome the inter-molecular forces of attraction. They become free to escape from the liquid surface and fly off as vapour. The heat absorbed during boiling does NOT increase kinetic energy (temperature stays constant). Instead, it goes into doing work against the forces of attraction and increasing the separation between molecules -- this increases the potential energy of the molecules.

IMPORTANT FACTS ABOUT BOILING:
- All liquids expand on heating.
- The boiling point of a liquid INCREASES when pressure is increased. This is the principle behind pressure cookers! Inside a pressure cooker, the pressure is higher than normal atmospheric pressure, so water boils at a temperature higher than 100 degrees Celsius. The food cooks faster because it is being heated at a higher temperature. This is also why cooking takes longer at high altitudes (like on mountains) -- the atmospheric pressure is lower there, so water boils at a temperature lower than 100 degrees Celsius.
- The boiling point of a liquid INCREASES when impurities are added. For example, adding salt to water raises its boiling point above 100 degrees Celsius.

key_terms.definition: The temperature at which a liquid changes into vapour (gas) without any further increase in temperature.
The process of a liquid changing into gas (vapour) on heating at a constant temperature (the boiling point).

tables.title: Boiling Points and Heat Absorbed for Common Liquids
headers: Heat Absorbed (Specific Latent Heat of Vaporization)

fun_facts: A pressure cooker raises the boiling point of water to about 120 degrees Celsius. That is why food cooks much faster in it!
On top of Mount Everest, the atmospheric pressure is so low that water boils at only about 70 degrees Celsius. You cannot make a proper cup of tea there because the water is not hot enough!
Steam at 100 degrees Celsius causes much worse burns than water at 100 degrees Celsius because steam releases a huge amount of latent heat (2260 x 10^3 J/kg) when it condenses on your skin.
examples: Water at 100 degrees C absorbs 2260 x 10^3 J of heat per kilogram to change into steam at 100 degrees C. During this entire process, the temperature remains constant at 100 degrees C.
Ether has a very low boiling point of only 35 degrees C, which is below body temperature. That is why ether evaporates almost instantly when it touches your skin, producing a strong cooling effect.

sections.title: Condensation
sections.content:
Condensation is the reverse of boiling. It is the process by which a vapour (gas) changes back into a liquid on cooling at a constant temperature. The temperature at which vapour changes into liquid without any further decrease in temperature is called the condensation point.

For example, when steam at 100 degrees Celsius is cooled, it releases heat and changes back into water at 100 degrees Celsius. The temperature stays at 100 degrees Celsius during the entire condensation process -- the heat released goes out to the surroundings.

IMPORTANT FACTS ABOUT CONDENSATION:
- For any substance, the boiling point and the condensation point are exactly the SAME temperature. Water boils at 100 degrees Celsius, and steam condenses at 100 degrees Celsius.
- The amount of heat released during condensation is exactly equal to the amount of heat absorbed during vaporization (for the same mass of substance). So if 1 kg of water absorbs 2260 x 10^3 J of heat to become steam, then 1 kg of steam releases exactly 2260 x 10^3 J of heat when it condenses back to water.

You can observe condensation in everyday life: water droplets forming on the outside of a cold glass of water, dew forming on grass in the early morning, and the fog on your bathroom mirror after a hot shower -- all of these are examples of water vapour in the air condensing into liquid water when it comes in contact with a cold surface.

key_terms.definition: The change of state from vapour (gaseous) state to liquid state on cooling at a constant temperature.
The temperature at which a vapour changes into liquid without any further decrease in temperature. It is the same as the boiling point for any substance.

fun_facts: Clouds form by condensation! Water vapour in the atmosphere rises, cools, and condenses on tiny dust particles to form water droplets -- which we see as clouds.
Your breath becomes visible on cold days because the water vapour in your warm breath condenses into tiny water droplets when it meets the cold air.
examples: Water droplets on the outside of a cold glass: water vapour in the surrounding air loses heat to the cold glass and condenses into liquid water.
Dew drops on grass in the morning: at night, the temperature drops and water vapour in the air condenses on cool surfaces like grass.

Steam from a boiling kettle disappearing as it moves away from the spout: actually, the visible 'steam' is tiny water droplets formed by condensation. True steam is invisible! The droplets form when hot water vapour meets cooler air near the spout.

sections.title: Evaporation
sections.content:
Evaporation is a special way in which a liquid changes into vapour. Unlike boiling, evaporation happens at ALL temperatures (not just at the boiling point), and it happens only from the SURFACE of the liquid (not throughout the entire liquid like boiling). Evaporation is a slow process, while boiling is rapid.

DIFFERENCES BETWEEN EVAPORATION AND BOILING:
Evaporation is a slow process; boiling is rapid. Evaporation takes place from the surface of the liquid only; boiling takes place throughout the entire mass of the liquid. Evaporation occurs at all temperatures; boiling occurs only at the boiling point. During evaporation, the temperature of the surroundings falls (cooling effect); during boiling, the temperature of the surroundings remains constant.

FIVE FACTORS AFFECTING THE RATE OF EVAPORATION:

1. TEMPERATURE OF THE LIQUID: Higher temperature means faster evaporation. On a hot summer day, wet clothes dry much faster than on a cool day. This is because at higher temperatures, more molecules have enough energy to escape from the liquid surface.

2. AREA OF EXPOSED SURFACE: Greater surface area means faster evaporation. A wet cloth dries faster when spread out on a clothesline than when folded up. When spread out, more surface is exposed, so more molecules can escape at the same time.

3. NATURE OF THE LIQUID: Volatile liquids (those with low boiling points) evaporate faster. Alcohol, spirit, and ether evaporate much faster than water because the inter-molecular forces in these liquids are weak, so molecules escape more easily. This is why volatile liquids are stored in tightly closed bottles.

4. FLOW OF AIR ABOVE THE LIQUID: Wind or airflow above the liquid surface speeds up evaporation. When you blow on hot milk or tea, it cools faster. The moving air carries away the molecules that have escaped from the surface, making room for more molecules to escape.

5. PRESENCE OF MOISTURE OR HUMIDITY IN AIR: Dry air allows faster evaporation than humid (moist) air. Wet clothes dry much faster on a dry summer day than during the rainy season. In humid air, there are already many water molecules in the atmosphere that make it harder for liquid molecules to escape from the surface.

MOLECULAR EXPLANATION OF EVAPORATION: In a liquid, molecules are constantly colliding with each other. During these collisions, some molecules near the surface gain enough kinetic energy to overcome the inter-molecular forces holding them in the liquid. These energetic molecules escape from the surface into the atmosphere, absorbing heat from their surroundings in the process. This is evaporation.

EVAPORATION PRODUCES COOLING: When molecules escape from a liquid surface, they take away energy from the remaining liquid and its surroundings, causing a drop in temperature. This is why evaporation produces a cooling effect.

When you pour alcohol or spirit on your palm, it feels cold because the alcohol evaporates rapidly, absorbing heat from your skin. An experiment confirms this: wrap a cotton ball around a thermometer bulb, pour some ether or alcohol on it, and watch the temperature reading drop.

key_terms.definition: The change of state from liquid to vapour that occurs at ALL temperatures from the SURFACE of a liquid. It is a slow process.
A liquid with a low boiling point that evaporates quickly, such as alcohol, spirit, or ether.
The amount of moisture (water vapour) present in the air. High humidity slows down evaporation.

tables.title: Evaporation vs Boiling
rows: Only at the surface of the liquid
Throughout the entire mass of the liquid
Takes place only at the boiling point (a fixed temperature)

fun_facts: Dogs do not sweat like humans. Instead, they pant to evaporate moisture from their tongues and mouths, which cools their bodies down!
The reason you feel cold when you step out of a swimming pool is evaporation. The water on your skin evaporates, absorbing heat from your body and making you feel chilly.

Desert water bags (like the traditional surahi or matka) are made of porous clay. Water seeps through the tiny pores and evaporates from the surface, cooling the water inside -- a natural refrigerator!
examples: Water in an earthen pot (surahi/matka) stays cool in summer: water seeps through the pores to the outer surface, evaporates, and absorbs heat from the water inside, cooling it.
Wet cloth strips placed on the forehead of a fever patient: the water evaporates, absorbing heat from the body and helping reduce the fever.
Hot tea poured into a saucer cools faster than in a cup: the saucer has a much larger surface area, so evaporation happens faster.
Our body maintains a temperature of 37 degrees Celsius (98.6 degrees F) through sweating: sweat evaporates from the skin surface, absorbing body heat and cooling us down.
We blow air over hot food or drinks to cool them: the airflow increases the rate of evaporation, which takes away heat and cools the food faster.

sections.title: Sublimation and Deposition
sections.content:
Most substances go through all three states -- solid to liquid to gas -- as they are heated. But some special substances skip the liquid stage entirely!

SUBLIMATION is the process by which a solid, when heated, directly changes into its vapour (gas) without first becoming a liquid. This happens in substances where the inter-molecular forces of attraction are weak. When heated, the molecules gain enough kinetic energy to completely break free from their positions and fly apart, going directly from the closely-packed solid arrangement to the widely-spread gas arrangement.

Examples of substances that sublime:
- Camphor -- the strong-smelling white substance used in pujas and as a moth repellent
- Ammonium chloride -- a chemical compound
- Iodine -- turns into beautiful purple vapour when heated
- Naphthalene -- used in moth balls (that is why moth balls slowly shrink and disappear when left in your cupboard, even without heating!)
- Solid carbon dioxide (dry ice) -- used for creating fog effects at concerts and in movies

DEPOSITION (also called solidification) is the reverse of sublimation. It is the process by which a vapour (gas) on cooling directly changes into a solid without first becoming a liquid.

For example, carbon dioxide gas, when cooled to extremely low temperatures, directly forms solid carbon dioxide called dry ice. When camphor vapour cools, it directly forms solid camphor crystals.

CLASSROOM EXPERIMENT: Place some camphor or ammonium chloride in a china dish. Cover it with an inverted glass funnel. Heat the dish gently. The solid will sublime -- it changes directly into vapour, and the vapours rise up. When these vapours touch the cool walls of the glass funnel, they undergo deposition -- they change directly back into solid, forming a layer of solid crystals on the inner walls of the funnel.

key_terms.definition: The process by which a solid directly changes into gas (vapour) when heated, without first changing into a liquid.
The process by which a gas (vapour) directly changes into a solid when cooled, without first changing into a liquid.
Solid carbon dioxide. It is called 'dry' ice because it sublimes directly into carbon dioxide gas without melting into a liquid.

fun_facts: Dry ice is solid carbon dioxide at a temperature of about -78.5 degrees Celsius. It is used to create the dramatic fog effects you see at concerts, parties, and in movies!

Naphthalene moth balls protect your clothes from moths and insects by slowly subliming into vapour. The vapour repels the insects. That is why moth balls shrink over time without leaving any liquid behind.
Frost forming on cold winter mornings is an example of deposition in nature -- water vapour in the air directly changes into tiny ice crystals on cold surfaces without becoming liquid water first.
examples: Moth balls (naphthalene) slowly shrinking and disappearing in your wardrobe: the naphthalene is subliming -- changing directly from solid to gas at room temperature.
Camphor disappearing when left in the open: camphor slowly sublimes into vapour, which is why you can smell it from a distance.

Dry ice creating fog at a party: the solid carbon dioxide sublimes into carbon dioxide gas, and the extreme cold causes water vapour in the air to condense into tiny water droplets, creating the fog effect.
Frost on grass on cold winter mornings: water vapour in the air undergoes deposition, directly becoming ice crystals on the cold grass surface.

sections.title: Latent Heat -- The Hidden Heat
sections.content:
One of the most important concepts in this chapter is latent heat. When a substance changes from one state to another, it absorbs or releases heat, but its temperature does NOT change during the process. This heat is called latent heat, which means 'hidden heat'.

But if the heat is being absorbed, why does the temperature not increase? Here is the explanation:

The average kinetic energy of molecules is what determines the temperature of a substance. When you heat a substance normally (without any change of state), the kinetic energy of molecules increases, and the temperature rises. But during a change of state, the absorbed heat does NOT increase the kinetic energy of molecules. Instead, it is used to do work against the inter-molecular forces of attraction -- it increases the SEPARATION between molecules. This increases the potential energy of the molecules, not their kinetic energy. Since kinetic energy does not change, the temperature does not change.

Similarly, when a substance changes state by releasing heat (like freezing or condensation), the released heat comes from the decrease in potential energy of molecules as they come closer together. The kinetic energy (and hence temperature) remains constant.

So remember: During change of state, absorbed/released heat changes POTENTIAL energy, not KINETIC energy. That is why temperature remains constant.

The amount of heat absorbed or released per unit mass during a change of state is called specific latent heat. It is different for different substances and different changes of state.

Key values to remember:
- Specific latent heat of fusion of ice: 336 x 10^3 J/kg (heat needed to melt 1 kg of ice at 0 degrees Celsius into water at 0 degrees Celsius)
- Specific latent heat of vaporization of water: 2260 x 10^3 J/kg (heat needed to convert 1 kg of water at 100 degrees Celsius into steam at 100 degrees Celsius)

Notice that the latent heat of vaporization (2260 x 10^3 J/kg) is much larger than the latent heat of fusion (336 x 10^3 J/kg). This makes sense because converting a liquid to a gas requires molecules to completely separate from each other and overcome all inter-molecular forces, while melting only requires them to become loosely arranged.

key_terms.definition: The heat absorbed or released during a change of state at constant temperature. It is called 'hidden' because it does not cause a change in temperature.
The amount of heat required to convert 1 kg of a solid into liquid at its melting point without any change in temperature. For ice, it is 336 x 10^3 J/kg.
The amount of heat required to convert 1 kg of a liquid into gas at its boiling point without any change in temperature. For water, it is 2260 x 10^3 J/kg.
The energy stored in molecules due to their position and separation from each other. Increases when molecules move apart (absorbing latent heat).

tables.title: Latent Heat Values -- Key Numbers to Remember

fun_facts: Steam burns are much more dangerous than boiling water burns! When 1 kg of steam at 100 degrees Celsius touches your skin, it first releases 2260 x 10^3 J of latent heat as it condenses to water, and THEN the hot water continues to burn you. That is a LOT of extra heat!
The latent heat of vaporization of water is almost 7 times the latent heat of fusion of ice. This is why it takes much longer to boil away a pot of water than to melt the same amount of ice.
examples: When ice melts at 0 degrees Celsius, it absorbs 336 x 10^3 J of heat per kilogram but stays at 0 degrees Celsius. This heat goes into increasing the potential energy of molecules by increasing the spacing between them.

When water boils at 100 degrees Celsius, it absorbs 2260 x 10^3 J of heat per kilogram but stays at 100 degrees Celsius. This heat overcomes the inter-molecular forces and allows molecules to become completely free.
When steam condenses to water, it releases exactly the same amount of heat (2260 x 10^3 J/kg) that was absorbed during vaporization. This is why steam radiators are effective heaters.
